comments.text: One can slightly improve Cambie's construction using Stef's theorem on consecutive divisors. Namely,\[ f(N)\ge {5N\over8}+{N\over(\log N)^{\beta+o(1)}}, \qquad \beta=1-\frac{1+\log\log3}{\log3}=0.004154751497404\ldots . \]Let \(A_0=\{n\le N/4:n\ {\rm odd}\}\cup[N/2,N]\). The identity \(1/a=1/b+1/c\iff (b-a)(c-a)=a^2\) shows that \(A_0\) is solution-free and \(|A_0|=5N/8+O(1)\). Call an odd integer \(a\) half-isolated if no divisor \(d\mid a^2\) lies in \([a/2,a)\). If \(E\subset(N/4,N/2)\) consists of half-isolated odd integers, then \(A_0\cup E\) is still solution-free: any new solution has \(a\in E\), so \(b,c\) are even and hence in \([N/2,N]\); then \(d=\min(b-a,c-a)\) satisfies \(d\mid a^2\), \(d<a\), and \(d\ge\max(N/2-a,a^2/(N-a))\ge a/2\), contradiction. For ordered divisors \(d_i(n)\), put \(r(n)=\min_i d_{i+1}(n)/d_i(n)\). Stef proved that \(S(x,2):=\#\{n\le x:r(n)\ge2\}\ge x/(\log x)^{\beta+o(1)}\), with the above \(\beta\). Let \(\mathcal H=\{m\ {\rm odd}:r(m)\ge2\}\). Every \(m\in\mathcal H\) is half-isolated, since \(d\mid m^2\) and \(d/m\in[1/2,1)\) would give \(d/m=u/v\) with divisors \(u<v\le2u\) of \(m\). The odd part of every integer counted by \(S(x,2)\) lies in \(\mathcal H\), and a fixed odd part \(m\) occurs at most \(1+\log_2(x/m)\) times. Thus, integrating against \(dx/x^2\),\[ \sum_{\substack{m\le X\\m\in\mathcal H}}{1\over m}\gg \int_3^X {S(x,2)\over x^2}\,dx\gg(\log X)^{1-\beta-o(1)}. \tag{1} \]Finally set \(E_N=\{mp:m\in\mathcal H,\ m\le N^{1/2}/10,\ p\ {\rm prime},\ N/(4m)<p<N/(2m)\}\). Then \(E_N\subset(N/4,N/2)\), and since \(p>2m\), each \(mp\) is half-isolated whenever \(m\) is; also the representation \(mp\) is unique. Hence, by the prime number theorem and (1),\[ |E_N|\gg {N\over\log N}\sum_{\substack{m\le N^{1/2}/10\\m\in\mathcal H}}{1\over m}\gg {N\over(\log N)^{\beta+o(1)}}. \]Therefore \(A_0\cup E_N\) is solution-free, proving the claim.
